title: Automated Detection of Code Smells Using Large Language Models
abstractText: Code smells are indicators of deeper design problems in software systems. This paper presents a novel approach to automated code smell detection leveraging large language models. We fine-tune a transformer-based model on a curated dataset of 15,000 labeled code snippets across five smell categories. Our approach achieves 92% precision and 87% recall on the benchmark dataset, outperforming existing static analysis tools by 18%. We validate our findings on three large-scale open-source projects.

title: A Systematic Survey of CI/CD Pipeline Optimization Techniques
abstractText: Continuous integration and continuous delivery pipelines are critical to modern software development. This survey systematically reviews 142 papers published between 2018 and 2025 on CI/CD optimization. We identify six major categories of optimization techniques including parallelization, caching strategies, and intelligent test selection. Our taxonomy provides practitioners with actionable guidance for improving pipeline efficiency.

title: DeepRefactor: Neural Program Repair for Complex Refactoring Operations
abstractText: Program repair techniques have traditionally focused on simple bug fixes. We present DeepRefactor, a neural approach capable of performing complex refactoring operations including extract method, move class, and inline variable. Our model uses a graph neural network to capture program dependencies and generates semantically equivalent refactored code. Evaluation on 500 refactoring instances from GitHub shows our tool successfully completes 73% of refactorings correctly.

title: MicroLint: A Static Analysis Tool for Microservice Anti-Pattern Detection
abstractText: Microservice architectures introduce unique design challenges. MicroLint is a lightweight static analysis tool that detects seven common microservice anti-patterns including god service, chatty communication, and shared database. The tool analyzes service definitions, API contracts, and inter-service communication patterns. In a controlled experiment with 12 industry microservice systems, MicroLint detected anti-patterns with 89% accuracy.

title: Experience Report: Adopting Test-Driven Development in a Legacy Banking System
abstractText: We report on our three-year experience of introducing test-driven development practices into a legacy banking system comprising 2.4 million lines of COBOL and Java code. We describe the incremental adoption strategy, the challenges encountered including resistance from senior developers and integration with existing testing frameworks, and the measurable improvements in defect density and development velocity achieved over the period.

title: Fault Localization via Spectrum-Based Analysis with Contextual Embeddings
abstractText: Spectrum-based fault localization uses program execution traces to identify likely faulty code locations. We enhance traditional SBFL techniques by incorporating contextual code embeddings that capture semantic relationships between program elements. Our approach re-ranks suspicious statements using a learned scoring function. Experiments on the Defects4J benchmark show a 24% improvement in top-5 accuracy over Ochiai and Tarantula baselines.

title: Privacy-Preserving Log Analysis for Distributed Systems
abstractText: System logs contain valuable debugging information but also sensitive data. We propose a differential privacy framework for log analysis that enables anomaly detection and root cause analysis while preserving user privacy. Our approach adds calibrated noise to log feature vectors before analysis. Evaluation on production logs from a cloud provider demonstrates that our method maintains 94% anomaly detection accuracy while providing formal privacy guarantees.

title: Quantum Computing Approaches for Combinatorial Software Testing
abstractText: Combinatorial testing generates test suites covering all t-way interactions of input parameters. We explore how quantum computing can accelerate covering array generation. Using quantum annealing on D-Wave hardware, we formulate covering array construction as a QUBO problem. Preliminary results on benchmark instances show quantum approaches can find competitive solutions for small to medium problem sizes, with potential for quantum advantage as hardware scales.

title: Green Software Engineering: Metrics and Measurement Frameworks
abstractText: As software's environmental impact grows, measuring energy consumption and carbon footprint becomes essential. We propose a comprehensive measurement framework for green software engineering encompassing code-level energy profiling, infrastructure utilization metrics, and lifecycle carbon accounting. We validate our framework through case studies with three software companies, demonstrating measurable reductions in energy consumption through targeted optimizations.

title: Flaky Test Mitigation: A Machine Learning Approach to Test Reliability
abstractText: Flaky tests undermine developer trust in test suites and waste CI resources. We present a machine learning classifier that predicts test flakiness based on code features, execution history, and environmental factors. Our model achieves 91% precision in identifying flaky tests before they manifest, enabling proactive mitigation. A six-month deployment at a large tech company reduced flaky test failures by 67% and saved an estimated 4,200 developer hours.

title: fortunate recall
abstractText:
Current LLM memory systems treat all personal facts identically—storing them in
flat vector stores or knowledge graphs with uniform retention—producing stores
that grow unboundedly while retrieval precision degrades. We present Fortu-
nate Recall, a composable policy layer that classifies personal facts into a 10+1
behavioral ontology and applies category-specific lifecycle policies: differential
temporal decay, slot-key supersession, event-time validity, and category-aware
retrieval routing. LLMs are used at ingestion and for a single lightweight candidate-
selection step at retrieval; all lifecycle operations—decay, supersession filtering,
event-time validity, category-aware scoring—execute as deterministic functions.
We formalize a lifecycle non-identifiability theorem showing that scoring functions
over semantic similarity and elapsed time alone cannot identify lifecycle state, and
prove that behavioral category, slot keys, lifecycle state, and event-time anchors
are individually necessary under the natural lifecycle metadata basis. We introduce
LifeMemBench, a 516-question temporal disambiguation benchmark across 40
synthetic personas with controlled supersession, preference changes, and logistical
expiry. FR-Bank, our infrastructure-independent implementation, achieves 77.3%
pass rate on LifeMemBench—outperforming Mem0 (61%), Memory-R1 (66.9%),
and MemoryOS (70.5%)—and 75.4% on the full 500-question LongMemEval-S
canonical benchmark under the exact Wu et al. (ICLR 2025) judge protocol
(79.5% on the 234-question oracle POC subset). End-to-end evaluation confirms
that lifecycle management reduces downstream confabulation from 45.1% to 22.4%,
an architectural gap that reasoning cannot close. Cross-generator validation on
Kimi K2.5 confirms invariance of this hierarchy. The ontology, benchmark, and
code are publicly released.

title: Fortunate Recall: Ontology-Driven Memory Lifecycle Management for Persistent Coherence in LLMs
abstractText: Current LLM memory systems treat all personal facts identically, storing them in flat vector stores or knowledge graphs with uniform retention, so stores grow unboundedly while retrieval precision degrades. The core challenge is lifecycle management: which memories should persist, which should be replaced, and at what rate, conditioned on the behavioral type of each fact. Fortunate Recall (FR) is a composable policy layer that classifies personal facts into a 10+1 behavioral ontology and applies category-specific lifecycle policies (differential temporal decay, slot-key supersession, event-time validity, and category-aware retrieval routing) as deterministic functions over LLM-extracted metadata. The lifecycle policy layer is pure math; the LLM is used at ingestion and for a single lightweight candidate-selection step at retrieval. FR-Bank, our infrastructure-independent implementation, achieves 77.3% pass rate on LifeMemBench, a new 516-question temporal-disambiguation benchmark, outperforming Mem0 (61%), Memory-R1 (66.9%), and MemoryOS (70.5%). FR-Bank also reaches 75.2% on the full 500-question LongMemEval-S canonical benchmark under the exact Wu et al. (ICLR 2025) judge protocol—to our knowledge, the first result evaluated under the original Wu et al. evaluation methodology. End-to-end evaluation reduces downstream confabulation from 45.1% (Mem0) to 22.4%, with cross-generator validation on Kimi K2.5 confirming the hierarchy is generator-invariant. The ontology, benchmark, and code are released as supplementary material and will be made publicly available upon publication.

title: Ontology-Driven Clustering for Extracting Legal Reasoning Graphs
abstractText: Extracting structured reasoning from long court judgments is a persistent challenge in legal NLP: large language models can identify individual legal concepts but struggle to consistently link them across thousands of tokens, producing graphs that are incomplete, structurally invalid, or non-reproducible. We present LEXCGRAPH, a pipeline that decomposes long-document graph extraction into a set of local inference problems by clustering extracted nodes around canonical legal concepts defined in a compiled ontology. Within each cluster, an LLM infers edges under ontology-provided constraints (definitions, AND/OR requirements, defeaters, relation whitelists); all cross-cluster structure is constructed deterministically. On 2,517 Indian Supreme Court cases from IL-TUR, ontology-normalized annotations outperform BM25 by +0.100 nDCG@10 (p < 0.05) and all dense retrieval baselines. Graph representations improve zero-shot outcome prediction by +11.1pp on Grok-4.1 and +11.1pp on Claude Sonnet 4.5 (McNemar p < 0.05), with substantially improved calibration (ECE: 0.109→0.026). Crucially, structured nodes without ontology-constrained edges perform worse than raw text, demonstrating that naive structuring destroys implicit textual signals—only ontology-constrained relational structure recovers and exceeds them. A leakage audit confirms comparable information access between representations (0.233 vs. 0.225 signals per 1K characters), and the consistent performance ordering across representations rules out LLM memorization. An ontology completeness ablation (11 vs. 54 concepts) reveals that enrichment dramatically improves graph quality (gold tier: 77%→90%, pseudo-cluster nodes: 93% reduction) while prediction accuracy remains unchanged—indicating that core doctrines saturate outcome-predictive signal while additional concepts improve structural fidelity for downstream applications. The AND/OR justification structure enables counterfactual impact analysis: across 15,276 justification sets, 77.5% of holdings are structurally fragile, and removing the single most critical concept eliminates one-third of a case's justifications on average. Cross-jurisdictional evaluation on ECHR cases confirms generalizability. We release the ontology, extraction code, and evaluation scripts.
